column1: Influenza Hospitalization Rates (per 100,000 population) for the FluSurv-NET Network (downloaded on Jun-30-2026)
Influenza Division - Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.
The Influenza Hospitalization Surveillance Network (FluSurv-NET) data are preliminary and subject
to change as more data become available. Hospitalization rates for the past seven weeks are
estimates. Because reporting is delayed, models estimate what rates would be if all data were
complete. Median estimated rates are shown as well as the estimated range reflecting uncertainty.
In particular, case counts and rates for recent hospital admissions are subject to reporting
delays. As data are received each week, prior case counts and rates are updated accordingly.
FluSurv-NET conducts population-based surveillance for laboratory-confirmed influenza-associated
hospitalizations in children and adults. The FluSurv-NET catchment area includes more than 90
selected counties or county equivalents in California, Colorado, Connecticut, Georgia, Maryland,
Michigan, Minnesota, New Mexico, New York, North Carolina, Oregon, Tennessee, Utah, and Washington.
Additional counties in Idaho (2010 to 2011), Iowa (2012 to 2013, 2020 to 2022), Ohio (2010 to
2025), Oklahoma (2010 to 2011), and Rhode Island (2010 to 2013) have also contributed to the
network. Incidence rates (per 100,000 population) are calculated using either the National Center
for Health Statistics' (NCHS) vintage bridged-race postcensal population estimates (for years prior
to 2020) or U.S. Census vintage unbridged-race postcensal population estimates (for 2020 through
present) for the counties or county equivalents included in the surveillance catchment area. The
rates provided are likely to be underestimated as influenza-associated hospitalizations might be
missed due to test availability and provider or facility testing practices.
seasons through 2024-25 season, except 2009-10 and 2021-22, case counts reflect cases hospitalized
during October 1 – April 30 of each influenza season. Prior to 2025-26, cases hospitalized during
Week 39 are included in the totals for Week 40, while cases hospitalized during Week 18 are
included in the totals for Week 17. In 2009-10, case counts reflect cases hospitalized during Sept
1, 2009 through April 30, 2010 (Week 35 –Week 17). In the 2021-22, rates reflect cases hospitalized
beginning October 1, 2021 (Week 39), but given late influenza season activity, the season was
extended to June 11, 2022 (Week 23). In 2023-2024, surveillance was extended to September 28, 2024
(Week 39) for situational awareness during the ongoing outbreak of highly pathogenic avian
influenza (HPAI) A(H5N1) virus among birds, poultry, dairy cattle, and other animals in the United
States (H5N1 Bird Flu: Current Situation Summary | Avian Influenza (Flu) (cdc.gov)). Starting with

the 2025-26 influenza season, case counts reflect year-round surveillance.
Influenza-associated
hospitalization rates by influenza A subtype are available for all seasons through 2024-25.
Influenza A subtype is missing for 38-72% of influenza A cases across seasons. Multiple imputation
procedures were performed over 70 datasets using logistic regression, with site, age group, and
admission month as predictors. In the 2021–22 season, >99% of influenza A cases in patients in
FluSurv-NET were A(H3N2); as such, in the 2021–22 season, missing influenza A subtype for
FluSurv-NET cases was assumed to be A(H3N2). Hospitalization rates were pooled across the 70
The dashed lines and shaded bands around the last 7 weeks of
data represent estimates of FluSurv-NET hospitalization rates, called “nowcasts .” The dashed line
shows the median estimate and the shaded bands show the 80% prediction interval (displayed as the
80% estimated range), meaning we expect the final reported hospitalization rates to fall within
this band 80% of the time. Nowcast models estimate weekly respiratory virus-associated
hospitalization rates for recent weeks before surveillance data are fully reported and while
hospitalization reports are still coming in. To do this, the models use information on the delay
between when a hospitalization occurs and when it is reported. These reporting delays are used to
estimate the fraction of reports expected within one, two, three, etc. weeks of hospital admission,
and to appropriately scale up incomplete recent reports to produce real-time estimates of the final
counts that will be observed once reporting is complete.
accurate when reporting delays are stable and predictable. Disruptions to reporting, e.g. an
unexpected system-wide outage or change in reporting policy at one site, can cause differences
between actual and expected reporting delays, which in turn can lead to inaccurate nowcasts. CDC
actively monitors FluSurv-NET nowcast accuracy, and past nowcasts have reliably predicted final
ensemble of predictions from three models: Baseline, GAM-Smooth, and GAM-RE. The Baseline model
uses the empirical distribution of past delays to project current delays, similar to the approach
of [1] . The GAM-Smooth is a generalized additive model (GAM) similar to [2]  that estimates
reporting delays using a smoothing spline with 3 knots, and (3) the GAM-RE is identical to the
GAM-Smooth, except that it estimates random effects to characterize the fraction of reports
received 0, 1, ... 7 weeks after the date of hospital admission. The GAM models are trained on the
most recent 10 weeks of data. The baseline model is informed by empirical delays observed between
October 2019 and April 2023, excluding June-Sept 2021 and May-Sept 2022 when FluSurv-NET
surveillance was not conducted.  The reported results are an unweighted probabilistic ensemble [3]
